what_manipulated: incidental exposure to money-related concepts
what_measured: endorsement of system-justifying beliefs and hierarchical social preferences
experiment: Units (college students) were randomly assigned to view stimuli containing money-related concepts or control concepts. Dependent variables included explicit agreement with system legitimacy statements, implicit associations measured via forced-choice or rating scales, and preference judgments. Main analysis used between-subjects t-tests or ANOVAs comparing money vs. control conditions across multiple outcome measures related to system justification.

what_manipulated: group membership and stereotype activation (female identity, math=male stereotype)
what_measured: implicit and explicit attitudes toward a domain; domain self-association
experiment: Units (college students, majority female) completed implicit association tests measuring domain-self and domain-gender associations, plus explicit attitude ratings. The analysis was correlational: stereotype strength (domain=male beliefs) and group identity (self=female) were regressed against attitude and identification outcomes. A mediational or moderation structure was tested rather than direct manipulation.

what_manipulated: anchor value magnitude (set at 15th and 85th percentiles of calibration distribution)
what_measured: quantitative bias in numerical estimation (distance from calibration baseline)
experiment: A calibration group provided estimates on uncertain quantities to establish baseline distribution. Test units then judged whether a predetermined anchor (high or low anchor) was above or below each target quantity before estimating. Anchored estimates were converted to percentile ranks relative to the calibration distribution. Anchoring effect size was measured as the difference in percentile placement between anchor conditions, permitting pooled analysis across problems.

what_manipulated: brief visual exposure to national symbol
what_measured: political party affiliation, voting intention, political beliefs, and implicit attitudes
experiment: Units (college students) were randomly exposed to national symbol imagery or control imagery in online experimental sessions. Political outcomes (voting intention, beliefs, implicit attitudes measured via IAT) were collected immediately and at follow-up timepoints (8 months and beyond). Primary analysis used between-subjects comparisons (t-tests or regressions) of symbol vs. control on political outcomes, with examination of effect durability at multiple time-points.

what_manipulated: range of response scale categories (low-range vs. high-range scale anchors)
what_measured: reported behavior frequency; comparative judgments and evaluations of behavior implications
experiment: Units (survey respondents) reported behavior frequency using one of two closed-response scales with different upper bounds (low-range: max 2.5 hours vs. high-range: max 4.5 hours). Between-subjects comparison examined whether scale-range affected reported behavior. Follow-up measures assessed inferred population norms and evaluations of the behavior's importance. Analysis used t-tests comparing behavior reports and evaluations between scale-range conditions.

what_manipulated: frame of decision problem (identical choice problem presented as gain vs. loss frame)
what_measured: preference reversals and choice patterns
experiment: Units (subjects) made choices between options in decision problems framed alternatively as potential gains or potential losses (or gains and losses of lives). Dependent variable was choice pattern (which option was selected). Between-subjects or within-subjects design compared frame condition on preference. Analysis documented reversals: options preferred in gain frame were dispreferred in equivalent loss frame, indicating frame-dependent preference reversal.

what_manipulated: cultural context (Hong Kong vs. United States participants); target location (north vs. south in fictional city)
what_measured: estimated likelihood of high/low status living in cardinal direction; personal location preferences
experiment: Units (college students in Hong Kong and US) estimated where high-status and low-status individuals live in a fictional city, or reported personal living preferences on spatial maps. Within-subjects design: each unit estimated locations for both status levels, or rated preference across cardinal directions. Between-groups comparison examined whether cultural group moderated the association between status and cardinal direction. Analysis used location estimates or preference ratings as outcome, regressed on status level and cultural group with interaction term.

what_manipulated: exposure to structure cues (reminders or displays of order in nature or society)
what_measured: willingness to engage in goal-directed action; motivation for action
experiment: Units (college students) were primed with structure cues (e.g., orderly patterns in nature or organized social systems) or control cues, then asked to rate willingness to pursue goals or engage in behavioral commitments. Between-subjects comparisons (ANOVA or t-tests) examined whether structure priming increased action willingness compared to control. The effect was expected to generalize across goal domains regardless of whether the prime was domain-related.

what_manipulated: incidental disfluency or difficulty cues (degraded font, difficult lettering, physical strain)
what_measured: reduced heuristic reliance; improved analytical reasoning and judgment accuracy
experiment: Units (college students) received reasoning or judgment tasks presented under fluent or disfluent conditions (e.g., clear vs. degraded font, normal vs. furrowed-brow posture). Between-subjects design: disfluency vs. fluency condition. Outcomes included judgment accuracy (heuristic errors), persuasion effects (central vs. peripheral processing), and syllogistic reasoning performance. Analysis used t-tests or ANOVAs comparing disfluency vs. fluency on reasoning accuracy and bias reduction.

what_manipulated: measurement method (explicit foundation relevance ratings vs. implicit moral judgments vs. taboo-trade-off reactions); political group membership (liberal vs. conservative)
what_measured: reliance on five moral foundation types (harm, fairness, loyalty, authority, sanctity)
experiment: Units (samples of adults varying in political affiliation) completed multiple measures of moral foundation reliance: abstract relevance ratings, moral judgments of scenarios, taboo trade-off reactions, and linguistic analysis of sermons. Primary comparison was between political groups (liberals vs. conservatives) on foundation endorsement across measurement methods. Analysis used descriptive statistics and/or ANOVA comparing groups; consistency of the group-by-foundation pattern across methods was examined.

what_manipulated: environmental cues or task framing related to consumer contexts (exposure to desirable goods, consumer vs. citizen framing, competitive context, resource-dilemma framing)
what_measured: materialistic concerns; negative affect; social orientation (competitiveness, selfishness); well-being
experiment: Units (college students) were exposed to environmental manipulations or task framings highlighting consumer contexts or control contexts, then completed outcome measures. Between-subjects design with multiple operationalizations (Exp 1: goods exposure; Exp 2: task label framing; Exp 3: competitive framing; Exp 4: resource dilemma). Dependent variables included self-report materialistic concerns, affect ratings, and behavioral measures (cooperation, competitiveness). Analysis used t-tests or ANOVAs comparing consumer-cue vs. control conditions.

what_manipulated: diagnosticity of behavior (behavior is diagnostic vs. minimally diagnostic of attitude); cultural context (American vs. Japanese participants)
what_measured: magnitude of correspondence bias (dispositional attribution to actor)
experiment: Units (American and Japanese college students) observed scenarios describing behavior that was socially constrained. The behavior was either diagnostic or minimally diagnostic of the actor's true attitude (within-subjects or between-subjects variation). Units made explicit judgments of the actor's attitude. Primary comparison was between cultural groups on the size of correspondence bias (attribution discounting due to constraints); mediational analysis examined online inferences made during judgment. Comparison used t-tests or regressions testing the cultural group by diagnosticity interaction.

what_manipulated: target behavior scenario (agent causes gay kissing vs. heterosexual kissing); disgust sensitivity (individual difference, not manipulated)
what_measured: perceived intentionality; implicit attitudes (IAT scores)
experiment: Units (college students) completed a disgust sensitivity questionnaire (individual difference measure), then made judgments about scenarios where behavior had side effects. In Study 1, units rated perceived intentionality of a side effect (gay or heterosexual kissing). In Study 2, units completed an implicit association test (IAT) measuring automatic associations with gay vs. heterosexual targets. Disgust sensitivity was regressed against outcome measures (intentionality, IAT bias) in correlational analysis, with separate models for gay vs. heterosexual conditions to test specificity.

what_manipulated: incidental anchor value present in environment (jersey number, product model number, restaurant name number)
what_measured: numerical estimate magnitude
experiment: Units (college students) made numerical estimates in domains where an incidental number was present (Exp 1: athlete jersey number; Exp 2: product model number; Exp 3: restaurant name number). Within-subjects or between-subjects design: high vs. low anchor value. Dependent variable was the numerical estimate (performance rating, sales proportion, spending amount). Analysis used t-tests comparing estimates between high and low anchor conditions, with examination of moderation by expertise or recall of anchor.

what_manipulated: age period (early adulthood, middle adulthood, old age); childhood family composition (number of siblings, sibling gender)
what_measured: social value orientation type (prosocial vs. individualistic vs. competitive); attachment security
experiment: Units (adults spanning age groups) completed measures of social value orientation (prosocial, individualistic, competitive classification or continuous scale) and reported attachment history. Study 1-2 examined correlation between attachment security and orientation. Study 3 examined childhood sibling count and gender as predictors of orientation. Study 4 examined age group differences in orientation prevalence. Analysis used correlations, chi-square tests, or regression models to test associations between predictors (attachment, siblings, age) and orientation outcomes.

what_manipulated: moral dilemma context (action vs. inaction, varying consequences for lives saved/lost)
what_measured: moral judgment pattern (consistency with principle of double effect); justification content
experiment: Units (web-based sample, diverse demographics) responded to moral dilemmas asking when harm is permissible, varying whether permissible option was action or inaction. Within-subjects: multiple dilemmas with same underlying structure. Dependent variables were judgment patterns (coded for consistency with theoretical principle) and open-ended justifications (coded for content). Analysis examined (1) whether aggregate judgments matched theoretical principle predictions, (2) demographic variation in judgments, (3) discrepancy between judgment patterns and post-hoc justifications.

what_manipulated: sociometric status (respect and admiration within face-to-face group); socioeconomic status (individual difference, not manipulated)
what_measured: subjective well-being (life satisfaction, positive and negative emotion frequency)
experiment: Units (samples of varying ages) completed measures of sociometric and socioeconomic status, and subjective well-being (life satisfaction, affect scales). Cross-sectional correlational design examined association between status types and well-being. Within-subjects or longitudinal designs tracked units over time, examining whether changes in sociometric status predicted concurrent changes in well-being. Mediation analysis tested whether effects of status on well-being operated through feelings of power and social acceptance. Multiple regression examined unique contributions of each status type.

what_manipulated: action type (tempt-fate actions vs. non-tempt-fate actions); outcome type (negative vs. neutral outcomes)
what_measured: perceived likelihood of negative outcomes; implicit accessibility of negative prospects
experiment: Units (college students) rated perceived likelihood of negative outcomes following actions described as tempt-fate vs. non-tempt-fate (Studies 1-2). Studies 3-5 examined mechanisms: negative outcomes were made more or less accessible depending on action type (via priming or semantic connection), and accessibility was measured (word completion, reaction time) or inferred from likelihood judgments. Mediation analysis tested whether heightened accessibility of negative outcomes mediated increased perceived likelihood following tempt-fate actions. Study 6 examined automatic processing via speeded response conditions.

what_manipulated: cultural context (U.S. American vs. Indian); action type (personal vs. interpersonal; ongoing vs. recalled; natural vs. controlled; mundane vs. important)
what_measured: whether action is construed as a choice (categorical or continuous judgment)
experiment: Units (American and Indian college students) read behavioral scenarios or made retrospective reports varying in interpersonality, temporality (ongoing vs. memory), locus of control, importance, and personal relevance. Within-subjects: units rated whether each behavior represented a choice. Between-groups comparison examined cultural differences in choice construal across action types. Analysis used proportion choosing or mean choice rating, tested between cultures via t-tests or mixed-model ANOVA with action type and culture as factors.

what_manipulated: cognitive task type and cultural context (East Asian, Asian American, European American); cognitive conflict (intuitive vs. formal reasoning strategy)
what_measured: reasoning approach preference (formal vs. intuitive strategy adoption)
experiment: Units (Chinese, Korean, Asian American, and European American university students) completed cognitive tasks (categorization, deductive reasoning, conceptual structure) designed to induce conflict between formal and intuitive reasoning strategies. Between-groups (culture) and within-subjects (task design) factors. Dependent variable was which reasoning strategy was used (formal vs. intuitive). Analysis compared strategy adoption rates or performance accuracy between cultural groups, examining whether formal-intuitive strategy conflict moderates cultural differences. Control conditions tested reasoning in absence of strategy conflict.
